Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine. I'd maybe open my laptop with good intentions, scroll through some materials, then end up watching YouTube or something. When I did study, it was usually late at night when I felt guilty enough to do something. I'd estimate maybe 20 hours a week total, but a lot of that was pretty unfocused and scattered.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then cram when a deadline was breathing down my neck. It wasn't intentional—I just couldn't make myself care enough to stick to a schedule. Other stuff always seemed more interesting or urgent, even when it wasn't really.
Q3: Usually panic about a deadline, or sometimes boredom when I'd already exhausted everything else on my phone. Occasionally I'd get an email reminder and feel a pang of guilt, so I'd log in just to see what I was behind on. It was rarely because I was genuinely curious about the content.
Q4: Not really, no. There was never a point where logging in felt natural or automatic. It always felt like a chore I was forcing myself to do. I'd tell myself I'd check it every morning, but that never lasted more than a day or two before I drifted back to ignoring it.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful because I'm a visual learner and could at least watch them passively. I watched quite a bit, actually—it felt easier than reading dense text. Sometimes the visuals helped concepts click in a way that reading never did for me.
Q6: The discussion forums were basically invisible to me. I posted maybe twice the whole semester and never really engaged with other students' posts. It felt pointless and awkward, like talking into a void. The readings I often skipped too—they were long and I'd lose focus after a few paragraphs.
Q7: If it was short, visual, and directly connected to what would be on the exam, I'd do it. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly going to help my grade just got pushed aside. I needed to see an immediate payoff, otherwise my brain just wouldn't cooperate.
Q8: Optional activities were basically non-existent to me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it—simple as that. Required stuff at least had consequences attached, which gave me enough pressure to sometimes follow through. Optional felt like a suggestion I could safely ignore.
Q9: Yeah, I'd download slides and PDFs and then study from those on my tablet without being online. Sometimes I'd take screenshots of important diagrams and review them later. I also used some YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials did.
Q10: A lot of my studying happened with downloaded materials or handwritten notes I made while watching videos. I'd rewatch my screenshots and diagrams without logging back in. So the LMS would show I watched a video once, but I might have reviewed that content multiple times offline.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a lecture video or the slides, then never go back to the LMS for that module. I preferred having things saved locally so I didn't have to deal with slow loading or logging in every time. It was just more convenient for my scattered study habits.
Q12: Around midterms, everything felt overwhelming and pointless at the same time. I was stressed but also just... bored? Like, I knew I should care more, but I couldn't make myself feel invested. The peer support thing they tried didn't really help because I wasn't connecting with anyone anyway.
Q13: Fatigue was huge—I'd be tired and just not have the mental energy to engage properly. Competing responsibilities like part-time stuff and just general life chaos meant the course kept getting deprioritized. When I was confused about something, I'd often just give up rather than seek help.
Q14: All the time. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd finish a video and realize I absorbed nothing. It looked like engagement from the outside, but internally I was just going through the motions.
Q15: I procrastinated on basically every assignment. I knew they were important, but starting felt impossible—like there was a wall between me and actually doing the work. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing. It's why my completion rate was so low.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore everything until the last possible moment, then scramble to submit something. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have done anything at all. They created artificial urgency that my brain apparently needed.
Q17: Feedback came too late to really matter, or I just didn't check it. When I did see it, I'd feel bad for a minute, maybe resolve to do better, then forget about it. It didn't change my behavior in any lasting way. I think I was too checked out to really process it.
Q18: The course had a lot of flexibility, which sounds good but was actually terrible for me. Without strict structure, I just drifted. If there had been more mandatory check-ins or smaller frequent deadlines, I might have stayed more on track. The freedom let me disappear.
Q19: The long gaps between deadlines made it easy to procrastinate. Also, the discussion requirements felt forced and awkward, so I avoided them. Having fourteen online courses to juggle was overwhelming—I couldn't give proper attention to any of them, including this one.
Q20: That sounds like me, honestly. You can click around a lot, watch videos, open files—but if you're not actually processing anything or completing assignments, it's just surface-level activity. Sometimes being in the LMS is more about feeling productive than actually being productive.
Q21: Yeah, I passed despite barely showing up online. I crammed before exams using my offline materials and managed to scrape through. The LMS activity doesn't show the desperate last-minute studying I did with downloaded content. Low activity doesn't always mean low learning, just different learning.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked things and how long you stayed, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning. They miss offline studying, mental engagement, and whether someone is struggling emotionally. Numbers don't capture the whole picture of what's happening with a student.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and actively thinking about it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being mentally present and curious. By that definition, I wasn't really engaged this semester. I was just surviving.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how bored and disconnected I felt most of the time. It wouldn't capture the stress I was under or how the peer support intervention didn't really reach me because I wasn't connecting with anyone. It also misses all the offline studying I did and the internal struggle of trying to make myself care.
